title: How to hold hope without making it into another performance
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
Hope as a performance I learned early

metaDescription:
Hope as a performance I learned early 

 I used to think hope was something I had to prove. I'd smile wider than I felt. I'd say "I'm fine" before anyone...
htmlContent:
Hope as a performance I learned early

I used to think hope was something I had to prove. I'd smile wider than I felt. I'd say "I'm fine" before anyone asked. I'd post quotes about healing on social media while sobbing into my pillow. Hope became another mask. Another way to be acceptable. Another way to survive.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in chaos, you learn that your real feelings are dangerous. You learn to perform the emotions that keep you safe. Happy. Hopeful. Fine. You become a virtuoso at pretending. And somewhere along the way, you stop knowing what you actually feel.

I remember sitting in a therapist's office three years ago. She asked me how I felt about my recovery. I said "hopeful" before I even checked in with my body. She just looked at me. Waited. I felt my throat tighten. My chest got heavy. I realized I had no idea what hope actually felt like in my nervous system. I only knew how to say the word. Right?

This is what happens when hope becomes a performance. You lose the ability to distinguish between genuine hope and the hope you're supposed to have. You start performing recovery instead of living it. You become the perfect patient, the model survivor, the person who "inspires" others. And inside, you're exhausted. Empty. Still scared.

Pete Walker, who wrote extensively about complex PTSD, calls this the "fawn response" in action. You're not actually feeling hope. You're fawning toward the idea of recovery itself. You're trying to be good enough for healing. Like healing is a reward you have to earn by being the right kind of hopeful person.

Stay with me here. This isn't about shaming yourself for performing hope. It's about recognizing that you learned this somewhere. It kept you safe once. It made sense in the environment where you grew up. But now it's getting in the way of real healing.

What hope actually feels like in the body

Let's get concrete. What does hope feel like when it's not a performance? I had to learn this from scratch. I'd ask my therapist "how do I know if I'm really hopeful?" and she'd say "what does your body tell you?" I hated that question. Because my body was just a blur of tension and numbness. I couldn't tell the difference between hope and fear.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about the vagus nerve and how safety is felt in the body before it's understood by the mind. Real hope, the kind that isn't performative, feels like a softening somewhere. Maybe your shoulders drop a little. Maybe your breath gets easier. Maybe you notice a small warmth in your chest. It's not dramatic. It's not a fireworks display. It's quiet. It's a tiny opening.

Performative hope feels tight. It feels like effort. Like you're holding something up. Like you have to keep it going or everything will fall apart. Real hope feels more like a sigh. Like a release. Like you can afford to let go for a second because something else is holding you.

Think about it. When you're performing hope, you're usually alone with it. You're doing it for an audience that may or may not exist. But genuine hope? It connects you to something outside yourself. To other people. To your own history of surviving things you thought would kill you. To the simple fact that your body kept breathing even when your mind had given up.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Walker breaks down how the fawn response works and how to start recognizing when you're performing safety instead of actually feeling it. It's not a light read. But it's honest. And honesty is what we need when we're trying to unlearn performance.

The problem with "toxic hope" and the wellness industry

I'm going to say something uncomfortable. The wellness industry has turned hope into a product. You're supposed to "choose hope." You're supposed to "" your recovery. You're supposed to "vibrate at a higher frequency" or whatever. This is not healing. This is spiritual bypassing dressed in yoga pants.

When you're recovering from complex trauma, you don't need more pressure to feel a certain way. You need permission to feel whatever is actually there. Grief. Rage. Numbness. Despair. All of it. Hope doesn't replace these feelings. It coexists with them. Sometimes it's barely visible. Sometimes it's completely absent. And that's okay.

Gabor Mate, the physician who studies trauma and addiction, often says that the opposite of trauma is not healing. It's connection. Real hope grows in connection. Not in isolation. Not in forced positivity. Not in "gratitude journals" that make you feel guilty for being sad. Real hope happens when someone sits with you in your pain and doesn't try to fix it. When you realize you're not alone. When you feel seen.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete exercises to track your nervous system states and notice when you're fawning toward hope versus actually feeling it. I found it helpful because it doesn't ask you to feel better. It asks you to notice.

Here's what I've learned. Hope that can't hold your despair isn't real hope. It's denial. Real hope is strong enough to include everything. Your anger at being hurt. Your grief for what you lost. Your fear that you'll never fully heal. All of it fits inside genuine hope. It doesn't require you to be positive. It just requires you to keep showing up.

How to practice imperfect hope

So how do you hold hope without making it into another performance? I don't have a perfect answer. But I have some practices that help me. I'll share them with the caveat that they might not work for you. And that's okay. Take what fits. Leave the rest.

First, check in with your body before you check in with your thoughts. When you catch yourself saying "I'm hopeful," pause. Feel your feet on the floor. Notice your breath. Is there any softening anywhere? Any opening? If not, that's information. You might be performing. You might be scared. You might be somewhere else entirely. Just notice. Don't judge.

Second, let hope be small. It doesn't have to be big. It doesn't have to be inspiring. It can be as small as noticing you made it through another day. It can be as small as choosing to eat something nourishing. It can be as small as reaching out to a friend when you wanted to isolate. Small hope is real hope. Big performative hope is usually a red flag.

Third, practice hope in relationship. Real hope grows when someone sees you struggling and doesn't run away. When you tell a trusted friend "I don't know if I can do this" and they say "I'll be here either way." That's hope. Not in the words. In the presence. A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Because hope without boundaries is just more performance for people who might not have your best interests at heart.

Fourth, get curious about your inner critic. That voice that tells you you're not hopeful enough, not positive enough, not healed enough. That's not hope talking. That's the part of you that learned to survive by performing. A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, shows how every part of you has good intentions. Even the part that performs hope. It's trying to keep you safe. You can thank it. And then you can let it rest.

Wild, right? The part of you that's been working so hard to perform hope might actually need to be told "you can stop now. I've got this. We're okay." That's where real hope starts. When you stop trying so hard.

Letting hope be messy

I don't have a neat ending for this. Hope is messy. It's inconsistent. Some days I feel it. Some days I don't. Some days I catch myself performing it and I have to stop and breathe and ask "what am I actually feeling?" And sometimes the answer is "I don't know" and that's okay too.

Janina Fisher, who wrote about trauma and the body, talks about how healing isn't linear. It's more like a spiral. You come back to the same places but at different depths. Hope works the same way. You might think you've dealt with performative hope, and then something triggers you and you're right back there. Smiling when you're dying inside. Saying "I'm fine" when you're not.

That's not failure. That's being human. That's having a nervous system that learned to protect you in a specific way. The goal isn't to never perform hope again. The goal is to notice when you're doing it. To have compassion for the part of you that's still trying to be safe. To gently remind yourself that you don't have to earn healing.

You don't have to be hopeful enough. You don't have to be positive enough. You don't have to be inspiring. You just have to be here. Breathing. Trying. Falling down. Getting back up. That's enough. That's always been enough.

So hold hope if you can. But hold it loosely. Let it be small. Let it be inconsistent. Let it coexist with everything else you're feeling. And if you can't hold hope today? That's okay too. Tomorrow is another day. And you're still here. That counts for something.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
